Breeding is chiefly by the Th $^{232}$ --U $^{233}$ cycle.
By 1944-45 Farrington Daniels had evolved designs for pebble-bed reactors (see Chapter 1, Section I, Part I) in which the fuel-moderator is pebbles of uranium carbide and graphite.
In 1944 he proposed the use of beryllium oxide in a high-temperature pile.
Later, a group at the Clinton Laboratories (now the Oak Ridge National Laboratory) continued development through 1947, when the concept was abandoned.
The fuel-moderator in the 1947 concept was a dispersion of uranium oxide in graphite or beryllium oxide, in the form of tubes.

For almost ten years after 1947, there was comparatively little work done on this type of reactor in the U.S., partly because of interest in water-cooled and liquid-metal-cooled reactors and also because of the view that the heat-transfer properties of gases would limit power density and specific power.
7, Journal of The Franklin Institute, May 1960, pp.
McCullough et al., Summary Report on Design and Development of High-temperature Gas-cooled Powcr Pile, MonN-383, Clinton Laboratories, Sept.
An early (1952) model of a solid homogeneous research reactor was the Table Model Reactor, described by Biehl, Hetrick, and Bennett.
The core radius is 11.8 cm.
Polyethylene has a somewhat higher hydrogen-atom density than does water (7.8 x 10 $^{22}$ nuclei/cm $^{3}$ versus 6.7 x 10 $^{22}$ for water).
The Homogeneous Graphite Reactor, described in 1954 by Stelle, $^{2}$ was intended both as a research tool and a source of service irradiations to produce isotopes.
The fuel, enriched uranium in $\mathrm{U}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{8}$ , is dispersed in graphite.
A power of $135 \mathrm{kW}(t)$ and an average neutron flux of $10^{12} \mathrm{neutrons/cm}^{2}/\mathrm{sec}$ were planned.
The ANCO-201 reactor (1955) is a compact, low-power reactor designed for training and research.

The originators, staff members of the Applied Nucleonics Corporation, stated that this reactor could be used with only 450 g uranium-235 and it is safe enough to allow it to be installed in highly populated areas.
The maximum thermal neutron flux is $6 \times 10^{6}$ neutrons/cm $^{2}$ /sec.
In 1956, Biehl et al. described AGN-201 as a compact, low-cost reactor designed for maximum safety and high sensitivity.
The core is divided into two halves separated by a fuse of polyethylene that has a high uranium-235 content.
Of negligible power, these reactors are cooled by natural convection, but they can be modified to operate at a maximum power of 20 MW(t).
The AGN-211 reactor $^{6,7}$ is similar in many ways to the AGN-201.
The reactor has a power of $100 \mathrm{~W}(t)$ .

The TRIGA reactors (Training, Research, and Isotope Production Reactor, General Atomic) introduced in 1958, have been widely used in the United States and abroad for training, irradiation, and isotope production.
There are four models; Mark I, Mark II, Mark III, and Mark F.[8-14] All have fuel-moderator elements of uranium mixed with zirconium hydride, and a coolant-shield of a pool of water.
In steady-state operation, these reactors are low power, but in pulsing they can produce $200\mathrm{MW(t)}$ .

In the Mark F, specifically designed for pulsing, the graphite reflector is eliminated; it has more fuel elements, which provide higher heat capacity; and it has a large water tank, for more versatility of radiation.[10] A large prompt negative temperature coefficient of reactivity is chiefly depended upon for safety, although control rods are provided.
An extrapolation of TRIGA technology for a reactor for powering a bathys- scaphe, research craft, or power buoy was suggested in 1960.15
In 1959, Thompson and Fahrner<sup>16</sup> described a design for a low-power research reactor in which the fuel-moderator is graphite impregnated with enriched uranium
as $\mathrm{U}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{8}$ and the coolant is heavy water.
The power is $200\mathrm{kW(t)}$ .

The authors indicated problems would be the large amount of heavy water needed (about 400 gallons); the limitation on temperature by the boiling of the heavy water; and difficulties in design of the cooling system, even at moderate pressures.
Siemens-Schuckertwerke A.G. announced in 1961 plans to build a solid homogeneous reactor as a prototype for a model to be offered for sale.[17] Few details were given.
The reactor is fueled and moderated with a suspension of powdered $\mathrm{U}_{3}\mathrm{O}_{8}$ in polyethylene and has a graphite reflector and shielding of lead and water.[18]

The homogeneous subcritical subassembly $^{16}$ designed by staff members at Aerojet-General Nucleonics is included here because it is intended for use in its own right to measure neutron fluxes, rather than as a step in designing another reactor.
It has a cylindrical core of a homogeneous mixture of $20\%$ enriched uranium and polyethylene and includes a neutron source.
Fuel-moderator: $93\%$ enriched U as $\mathrm{UO}_2$ mixed homogeneously with polyethylene.
Re-lector: BeO and graphite. $\mathrm{UO}_2$ particles, not more than $10^{\circ}\mu$ in size; mixed with powdered polyethylene and pressed in a mold.
Impregnation density: $50~\mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{U/cm}^3$ .
Reflector: 2 ft graphite around core.
Core tank: cylinder 46 in. diam., 93 in. high.
Average thermal flux: $10^{12}$ neutrons/cm²/sec.
Core will last 20,000 hours.

Details: Extrapolation of TRIGA technology for small reactor producing 25-50 kW(e) to deliver 35-70 hp for powering a bathyscaphe, research craft, or power buoy for the U.S.
Core: hexagonal prism $4\frac{1}{2}$ ft high.
37 Al-clad, helium-filled fuel elements; each contain hexagonally shaped fuel-moderator blocks.

Faris<sup>1</sup> discussed graphite.
Jaye et al.,<sup>2</sup> and Roberts,<sup>3</sup> gave the advantages of beryllium oxide.
Jaye et al.<sup>2</sup> have suggested that a beryllium oxide spine might be used in a moderator element of graphite.
Studies of the use of beryllium oxide in a high-temperature power reactor were begun by Farrington Daniels in 1944 at the Metallurgical Laboratory, University of Chicago.
the Metallurgical Laboratory until 1946, when this work was transferred to a group at the Clinton Laboratories.
Emphasis was put on a design that could be developed in a fairly short time.<sup>4</sup>
In November 1946, a preliminary design was proposed.

In this 40 MW(t) reactor, the fuel-moderator is in the form of enriched uranium oxide mixed with beryllium oxide to form cylinders, which are within axial holes in hexagonal prisms of beryllium oxide.
This study was published in February 1947.
The power is the same as for the oxide reactor, $40\mathrm{MW(t)}$ .

In June 1947 the USAEC notified the Laboratory that it would not authorize design or construction of the power pile, but investigations on problems should continue at a lower priority.<sup>4</sup> In September of the same year, the Power Pile Division (the Clinton Laboratory group assigned to the project), issued a final report summarizing the status of the development.<sup>4</sup> The design advanced in this report is generally similar to that for the earlier design with beryllium oxide.
The power is lower, $12 - 20\mathrm{MW}(t)$ , and beryllium metal or graphite are considered
These concepts differed from the 12-20 MW(t) concept chiefly in dimensions, fuel loadings, and engineering.
A 1950 concept by Daniels, the Impregnated Graphite, Nitrogen-Cooled Reactor, was designed to use the standard equipment and materials available in 1950.
The reactor was designed as a breeder, with fully enriched uranium to be used for breeding $\mathbf{U}^{233}$ and slightly enriched uranium for breeding plutonium.
For breeding $\mathbf{U}^{233}$ , thorium nitrate would be in the fuel-moderator or in the graphite reflector.
Plutonium would be bred from the $\mathbf{U}^{238}$ in the fuel.
The coolant flows from the top of the core to the bottom through channels in the graphite blocks and exits at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The maximum power is 11.5 MW(t).
Another concept by the same author in 1956 was the Gas-Cycle Reactor, in which the fuel-moderator consists of uranium carbide, which could be in different forms, such as rods or pellets.
This reactor was also designed for high temperatures, with an exit coolant temperature of $1350^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Siegel, in 1951, suggested a power breeder reactor in which the fuelmoderator is graphite impregnated with uranium dicarbide.
The power is $1400\mathrm{MW}(t)$ .

Investigations at the Studebaker-Packard Corporation, reported by Thompson in 1956, $^{10}$ resulted in a design for a gas-cooled reactor, with solid homogeneous fuel moderator, intended for use with a gas turbine power plant.
The power is $60\mathrm{MW(t)}$ .
In a 1960 patent, applied for in 1956, $^{11}$ Fortescue and Lockett described a reactor with solid homogeneous fuel-moderator elements.
Inoue et al.12 in 1958 proposed that enriched uranium oxide, impervious graphite, and thorium oxide be incorporated into elements for a breeder reactor with a power of $600\mathrm{MW(t)}$ .
The outlet temperature of the coolant gas $(700^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , according to the authors, would make possible use of the reactor with a gas or steam turbine.
The Semi-homogeneous Critical Experiment (SHE) $^{13-15}$ was reported in 1961 by Inoue and co-workers.
The fuel-moderator is a uniform mixture of $20\%$ -enriched $\mathrm{UO}_2$ and graphite, formed into discs.
The first loading was in 1961, and operation of the experiment was continuing in 1964.
The Semi-homogeneous Gas Cooled Breeder Reactor (SHR) is very similar to the critical experiment, but it is designed for a power of 25 MW(t) and is cooled by helium.
The fuel-moderator, $\mathrm{UO}_2$ or $\mathrm{UC}_2$ particles smaller than $6\mu$ evenly dispersed in graphite, is in the form of pellets.

The Peach Bottom (Pennsylvania) High-temperature Gas-cooled Reactor (HTGR) $^{17-24}$ utilizes a design intended to have the advantages of high outlet temperatures for the gas coolant ( $1360^{\circ}\text{F}$ or higher); high fuel burnup; high power density; and good neutron economy.
The preliminary design and development of the HTGR began early in 1957 at General Atomic Division, General Dynamics.
The project is supported by High Temperature Reactor Development Associates, a group of 52 private utility companies.
Construction began in 1962.
Fuel loading and startup were scheduled for early 1965 but were delayed by a fire in the containment
shell. $^{24}$ The reactor went critical on March 31, 1966.
Power operation is now scheduled for late 1966.
The reactor, designed as a converter, utilizes $U^{235}$ as initial fuel and will use $U^{233}$ after the breeding cycle has begun.
The power is 115 MW(t); 40 MW(e).

Advanced forms of the Peach Bottom reactor are under consideration $^{19,25}$ a 300- to 500-MW(e) reactor is the subject of a research and development program being carried out jointly by General Atomic and the Empire State Atomic Development Associate, Inc.

With the same type of fuel elements as are in the Peach Bottom plant, the advanced design is for a reactor to produce steam for a 2400 psi, $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ superheat, $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ reheat steam cycle.
A modified form of the Peach Bottom fuel is being evaluated for a 1000-MW(e) reactor plant, the Thermal, Advanced Reactor, Gas-cooled, Exploiting Thorium (TARGET).
This concept is being considered for possible construction in the 1970's.

The reactor would have about the same surface temperatures as in the Peach Bottom prototype, but a more efficient steam cycle would be used, to give a steam pressure of 3500 lb psi, superheat to $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and two reheats, each to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
In a suggested alternative, features are the same except that the fuel-moderator-fertile material consists of microscopic particles ( $1\mu$ or less in diameter) dispersed in a graphite matrix.
Still another possibility being evaluated is operation with recycled $\mathbf{U}^{233}$ as fuel and thorium fertile material in the fuel-moderator elements to achieve core breeding.

The OECD High Temperature Gas-Cooled Reactor Project (DRAGON), $^{26-28}$ had its origin in about 1956, when a team of the United Kingdom Atomic Energy Authority, under Fortescue, began preliminary studies.
The first report was the 1958 paper by Shepherd et al. $^{26}$ In that year, a committee was set up under the auspices of
In 1959 an agreement was concluded by Austria, Denmark, Euratom, Norway, Sweden, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom for work on this type of reactor.

The objectives of this reactor development are those of others utilizing solid homogeneous fuel, including the attainment of exit-gas temperatures $(1382^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ higher than possible before.
The core, which is made up of 37 of these fuel elements in a triangular lattice, is surrounded by a graphite reflector.
The power is $20\mathrm{MW(t)}$ .

The Experimental Beryllium Oxide Reactor (EBOR) is the land-based prototype for the Maritime Gas-cooled Reactor (MGCR), which is to be of higher power, but otherwise based on EBOR.[29-31] The purpose is to demonstrate the technology of beryllium oxide as moderator for gas-cooled reactors to be used in central station and marine power plants.
The exit temperature is $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The design of EBOR is for 10 MW(t).
The Ultra High Temperature Reactor Experiment (UHTREX),<sup>32-37</sup> formerly termed TURRET, is being developed at Los Alamos Scientific Laboratory, with completion scheduled for 1966.
The design is for a coolant exit temperature $(2400^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ appreciably higher than any previously used.
to investigate operation at high temperature, e.g., the behavior of unclad porous graphite fuel elements at up to $3000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The power for this experiment is 3 MW(t).

The use of beryllium oxide as moderator in a high-temperature pebble-bed reactor (the HTGC, High Temperature Gas Cooled Reactor) is being investigated by workers of the Australian Atomic Energy Commission.38-41 Beryllium oxide is the matrix for a solid ceramic fuel-moderator-fertile material, in which plutonium and thorium oxides are dispersed.

The study of beryllium-moderated, high-temperature, gas-cooled reactors was begun by the Australian AEC in 1960, and the concept is being assessed now for plutonium fuel; U233 and U235 fuels will be considered later.
The carbon dioxide coolant flows up between the core vessel and the pressure vessel to the top then downward through the core, leaving the bottom at $700 - 800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The power density is 10-20 kW/liter.

A brief description of a helium-cooled solid homogeneous reactor was published in 1963 by Sternglass et al.42 The reactor is part of a system designed to utilize MHD (magnetohydrodynamic) power generation.
In the 264-MW(e) reactor, the core is a graphite cylinder, in which fuel-moderator elements, of uranium carbide uniformly distributed in graphite, are contained within vertical channels.
In a 1960 United Kingdom Patent, $^{43}$ applied for in 1956, the fuel, moderator, and fertile material are combined as a mixture to form fuel rods.
A typical mixture would be $\mathrm{U}^{233}\mathrm{O}_2$ , BeO, and $\mathrm{ThO}_2$ .

A low-power [about $100\mathrm{kW(e)}$ ] reactor is being developed at the Martin Nuclear Division of the Martin-Marietta Company for a direct-conversion system, Terrestrial Unattended Reactor Power System (TURPS).44,45 The system is for a self-regulating constant heat source at about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
A plant with a net power of 1.3 MW(e) was originally proposed.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: In 1959 patents, filed in 1949, $^{46,47}$ Grebe described a reactor for propulsion in which the porous fuel-moderator element is in the form of porous segments of fuel-moderator, separated by coolant passages.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: of this core, the name "Cabbage Head" has been applied to it.48 The segments converge to close at one end and are joined to adjacent segments at the other end.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: An early (1952) concept by staff members at the Bendix Corporation is for a low-power water-cooled reactor in which $U^{235}$ is distributed in zirconium hydride.49

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: A design for a high-temperature reactor utilizing a ceramic of highly enriched uranium dioxide in beryllium oxide with yttrium hydride (which has a much higher hydrogen content than zirconium hydride) as additional moderator was proposed by Leeth in 1958.[50] The $\mathrm{UO}_2$ -BeO and the yttrium hydride are arranged homogeneously in concentric polygons.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The coolant, air, has an exit temperature of between $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and $2000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: In 1960, Levengood, Dissler, and Kalinowski described a reactor, the PWAR-II (Pratt & Whitney Aircraft Reactor - II), in which the fuel-moderator is uranium dioxide in beryllium oxide.[51] Lithium coolant flows around the hexagonal containers for the fuel-moderator pins.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Designs for 510 MW(t) and 200 MW(t) reactors were given.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Newgard and Leval described in 1960 a design for rocket propulsion in which uranium-loaded graphite plates form a core through which hydrogen propellant flows.[52] The reflector is made up of beryllium oxide blocks, and control is by movement of control plates in the reflector.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: As part of the PLUTO program to develop a reactor for a nuclear ramjet engine, two solid homogeneous reactors, Tory IIA-1 and Tory IIC, were developed and successfully tested.[53,54] The reactors are similar in design.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: In both, the cylindrical core consists of bundles of many fuel-moderator elements (100,000 for Tory IIA-1 and several hundred thousand for Tory IIC) of highly enriched uranium dioxide homogeneously mixed with beryllium oxide.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Tory IIA-1 first operated on May 14, 1961.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Full power, 155 MW(t), operation took place later in 1961.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The operation of Tory IIC (May 20, 1964) at full power was also successful.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Work on the SNAP (Systems for Nuclear Auxiliary Power) $^{55-60}$ reactors originated in the need in advanced space vehicles for compact, long-lived, and high-power nuclear sources of electrical power.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Atomics International, a division of North American Aviation, Inc., began investigating the problem in 1953, and by 1955 the type of reactor was chosen.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: In the general reactor design, the fuel is $U^{235}$ , moderated with zirconium hydride.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: In 1956, the USAEC established the SNAP program.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The general design was used in a series of reactors: SNAP Experimental Reactor (SER); SNAP-2 Development Reactor (SDR or S2DR); SNAP-2; SNAP-4; SNAP-8; SNAP-10; and SNAP-10A.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The first two were preliminary developments for the SNAP-2, SNAP-8, SNAP-10, and SNAP-10A reactors.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: SNAP-2 was intended as a low-power $[3\mathrm{kW(e)}]$ reactor for space application for use with a mercury Rankine power conversion cycle; it was a predecessor of SNAP-8.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: This project was redirected in 1963, although the Rankine cycle was used in later developments.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: SNAP-8 is a joint project between the USAEC and the National Aeronautics and Space Administration for developing a reactor of $600\mathrm{kW(t)}$ , $35 - 50\mathrm{kW(e)}$ , for space applications with a mercury Rankine cycle.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: SNAP-10 and SNAP-10A are direct-conversion systems.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: In SNAP-10, the heat is converted by conduction from the core to a thermoelectric system.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The SER,55,56 50 kW(t), operated from September 1959 until the end of the test program, in November 1960.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: During more than a third of the operating time the outlet temperature of the coolant was $1200^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ .

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: SNAP-2 Development Reactor (S2DR, SDR) $^{55-57}$ was a second-generation design, which was to approach the requirements of a space reactor.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: It operated successfully from April 1961 until December 1962.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: A prototype of SNAP-8, $^{55-57}$ with a higher power, $600\mathrm{kW(t)}$ , was undergoing test operation in 1965.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The design for SNAP-10 $^{58,59}$ incorporates cooling by direct conduction of heat from the core to thermoelectric devices on the reactor face.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Air Force for a device to produce 200-250 Watts (electrical).

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: In 1958-59 Atomics International developed this concept, with work continuing until 1960.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Some reasons for redirecting the project were: the temperature range desired $(1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ was too high for the thermoelectric material then used (lead telluride); and there was little prospect of the solid-fin-type heat-rejection radiator being developed to increase the power much beyond 300 Watts.

text: a liquid-metal coolant, as used in the SNAP-2 reactor, the higher powers are possible.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: SNAP-10A $^{56,57,59}$ is essentially the SNAP-2 concept with lower power, 34 kW(t), and lower outlet temperature for the coolant, $1010^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ instead of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The conversion system has an overall efficiency of $1.63\%$ .

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Work on this reactor was begun in 1961 and performance testing was begun in December 1962.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: A SNAP-10A reactor (FS-3) operated in a ground test for 10,000 hours from January 1965 to March 1966.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: On April 3, 1965, another SNAP-10A (FS-4) was orbited in a spacecraft.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: It operated until May 16, 1965, when the system shut down.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: SNAP-4 differs from the other SNAP reactors in purpose and in the coolant used.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: A project on this reactor began in 1959, with the aim of developing a reactor and turboelectric system that would be very compact and capable of long unattended operation under water or in a remote land location.[61] The power level is 1-4 MW(e).

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The design is very similar to that of the SNAP-2 and SNAP-8.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The fuel-moderator is hydrided zirconium-uranium alloy as rods in bundles that make up a vertical hexagonal core.[62] Unlike the other SNAP reactors, SNAP-4 utilizes boiling water as coolant.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Advantages claimed for the design are compactness; feasibility (built with existing technology); low cost; safety; no maintenance during core life; versatility in application; and automatic, unattended operation.[62]

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Under Project Rover, which began at the Los Alamos Scientific Laboratory in 1955, $^{63}$ development of solid-fueled reactors as potential sources of flight power was begun.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The reactors would produce high temperatures and would pose no unexpected problems in startup, stable operation, or shutdown. $^{64}$

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The first integral reactor test was of Kiwi-A.64-66 This reactor was successfully tested on July 1, 1959.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: A power of 70MW(t) and an exit-gas temperature of $3200^{\circ}\mathrm{R}$ were achieved.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: After the testing of Kiwi-A, several designs were developed and tested, with the aim of producing a 1000-MW(t) reactor.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Even the early Kiwi tests showed the potential of such reactors and stimulated interest in developing a flight engine.[63]

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: In the development of the Kiwi reactors, Kiwi-Bl was designed for liquid hydrogen, but the first Kiwi-B reactor that was operated (Kiwi-B-lA) utilized gaseous hydrogen.[65] It was tested on December 7, 1961, attaining a power of $330\mathrm{MW(t)}$ .

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The test of Kiwi-B-lB (1962) was the first test in which the reactor was started and brought to high power with liquid hydrogen as coolant.[67] Testing continued, with Kiwi-B-4E reaching $1000\mathrm{MW(t)}$ .

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The power for the test of this reactor (August and September 1964) was nearly $900\mathrm{MW(t)}$ , and the temperature of the coolant leaving the fuel elements was about $4000^{\circ}\mathrm{R}$ .[65] Among the developments in the Kiwi-B series was the demonstration that the reactors could be controlled by drums in the reflector.[68]

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: The reactor, NRX (NERVA Reactor Experiment), was given a non-nuclear test (NRX A-1) in the spring of 1964 to test stability and structure.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: NRX-A2, a nuclear test, was successfully carried out in September and October, 1964.[63]

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Control is partly by 12 cylindrical beryllium drums, within the outer reflector, that rotate to turn a poison strip to or away from the core.

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: There are also control rods in the core and the outer reflector.[69]

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Phoebus-1 operated in July, 1965 at its design power, $1000\mathrm{MW(t)}$ .

section: Reactors for Aircraft and Space Vehicles
text: Performance parameters of the Phoebus-2 design illustrate some of the conditions sought: a power of $5000\mathrm{MW(t)}$ , a hydrogen flow of 285 pounds per second, and an exit-gas temperature (from the fuel elements) of $4500^{\circ}\mathrm{R}$ .

517 channels in core: 504 for fuel elements, 13 for safety and control rods.
